content.text: The system prompt is the agent's stable briefing. Unlike the
list — which grows and changes on every step — the system string is passed unchanged to every
call throughout the agent run. It sets the model's role, establishes constraints, describes the environment, and specifies the output format. Everything the model needs to know that is
specific to the current step belongs here.

attrs.alt: System instruction structure — six sections and their roles

content.text: A well-structured system prompt has up to six sections. The first five are stable across the run; the sixth is optional and updated per call:
Who the model is and what its goal is
Hard rules the model must not violate
How the model should structure responses
When and in what order to use tools

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# The system prompt is a single string passed to every messages.create call.
# It is separate from the messages list — it never counts against trimming
# and does not break the alternating-role invariant.
system = """
You are a coding agent for a Python web service.
Your goal is to fix bugs without changing test files.

Constraints:
- Never modify any file in the tests/ directory.
- Keep changes minimal — fix only what is broken.
- Do not install new packages without explicit approval.

Environment:
- Repository root: /workspace/myapp
- Entry point: src/app.py
- Test suite: pytest tests/

After each tool call, briefly explain what you did and why.
When you are done, summarise all changes in one paragraph.
"""

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=4096,
    system=system,
    tools=[],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Fix the login bug reported in issue #42."}],
)
Pass system= to every create call
— the system prompt is not part of the messages list. It does not count against the alternating-role invariant and is not trimmed.
— the Anthropic API caches stable system prompts. A prompt that changes on every call is not cached, adding input token cost on each step.
. Paths, directory names, and version numbers ground the model.
A builder function assembles the system prompt from typed components, making it easy to vary behaviour per agent configuration without editing a raw string:
Each section is a separate argument
— constraints are a list, environment is a dict. This makes it easy to add or remove pieces without string manipulation.
Optional sections are omitted when empty
— output_format and tool_guidance are skipped if not provided, keeping the prompt lean.
The return value is a plain string

Some information changes on every loop step — the current scratchpad summary, for example. Appending this as a clearly labelled section at the end of the system prompt keeps the stable prefix cacheable:

def build_system_with_state(base_system: str, scratchpad_summary: str) -> str:
    """
    Append a compact dynamic state section to the stable base system prompt.
    The base_system portion is cached by the Anthropic API.
    The dynamic section is appended fresh on each call.

    Keep the dynamic section short — 200–500 characters is typical.
    Long dynamic sections degrade caching effectiveness.
    """
    if not scratchpad_summary or scratchpad_summary == "Scratchpad: empty":
        return base_system
    return f"{base_system}\n\n--- Agent State ---\n{scratchpad_summary}\n---"


# In the agent loop:
# for step in range(MAX_STEPS):
#     system = build_system_with_state(SYSTEM, scratch.summary())
#     response = client.messages.create(system=system, ...)
Append dynamic state after the stable sections, not before. The API cache matches on prefix — the more stable content appears first, the better the cache hit rate.
The quality of the system prompt directly affects agent behaviour. Vague instructions produce hallucinated details; ambiguous constraints are violated. Specific, concrete language works better:

# VAGUE — model will hallucinate the details
BAD_ROLE = """
You are a helpful AI assistant that helps with code.
"""

# SPECIFIC — gives the model a clear frame of reference
GOOD_ROLE = """
You are a backend engineer debugging a production incident on a Python FastAPI service.
The service handles payment processing. Your job is to identify the root cause of
a 500 error appearing in the /checkout endpoint and propose a minimal fix.
"""

# AMBIGUOUS CONSTRAINTS
BAD_CONSTRAINTS = [
    "Be careful with the database.",
    "Don't break things.",
]

# EXPLICIT CONSTRAINTS
GOOD_CONSTRAINTS = [
    "Do not execute any SQL that modifies data (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP).",
    "Do not commit or push git changes.",
    "Run tests after any file modification and stop if tests fail.",
]

# VAGUE OUTPUT FORMAT
BAD_FORMAT = "Explain your work."

# ACTIONABLE OUTPUT FORMAT
GOOD_FORMAT = (
    "After each tool call, write one sentence: what you did and what you found.\n"
    "When you have identified the bug, write: ROOT CAUSE: <one sentence>.\n"
    "When you have applied a fix, write: FIX APPLIED: <one sentence describing the change>."
)
. The model behaves more precisely when given a concrete frame.
"Do not execute SQL that modifies data"
— specify the exact prefix for key outputs (
) so they are easy to parse programmatically.

# System prompt length guidelines
#
# Recommended lengths by section:
#   Role / persona:      1-3 sentences (50-150 tokens)
#   Constraints:         3-8 bullet points (80-200 tokens)
#   Environment:         3-6 key-value pairs (60-150 tokens)
#   Output format:       2-4 sentences (50-100 tokens)
#   Tool guidance:       2-6 bullets (50-150 tokens)
#   Dynamic state:       1-5 lines (50-200 tokens)
#
# Total recommended range: 300-900 tokens
#
# Why keep it concise?
# - Every system prompt token appears in every call's input.
# - A 2000-token system prompt on a 30-step run costs 60 000 extra input tokens.
# - Long prompts dilute the signal — the model's attention is finite.
# - Stable prompts under ~1024 tokens are cached by the Anthropic API.
#
# When a system prompt grows over 900 tokens, review each section:
# - Remove examples (put them in the task description instead)
# - Remove repetition (say each constraint once)
# - Move environment details to a scratchpad if they change per call

def measure_system_prompt(system: str) -> dict:
    """Rough token estimate for a system prompt string."""
    tokens = int(len(system) * 0.25)
    return {
        "chars": len(system),
        "approx_tokens": tokens,
        "status": "ok" if tokens < 900 else "consider trimming",
    }
— shorter prompts are cached more reliably and leave more of the context window for tool results.
— repeating rules dilutes attention. State each constraint exactly once, clearly.
Examples go in the task, not the system prompt
— if you want to show the model an example, put it in the initial user turn, not the system prompt. It is task-specific, not always-applicable.

import pytest


class TestBuildSystemPrompt:
    def test_contains_role(self):
        system = build_system_prompt(
            role="You are a coding agent.",
            constraints=[],
            environment={},
        )
        assert "coding agent" in system

    def test_constraints_formatted(self):
        system = build_system_prompt(
            role="You are a coding agent.",
            constraints=["Never modify tests.", "Keep changes minimal."],
            environment={},
        )
        assert "- Never modify tests." in system
        assert "- Keep changes minimal." in system

    def test_environment_included(self):
        system = build_system_prompt(
            role="You are a coding agent.",
            constraints=[],
            environment={"Root": "/workspace", "Python": "3.12"},
        )
        assert "/workspace" in system
        assert "3.12" in system

    def test_optional_sections_excluded_when_empty(self):
        system = build_system_prompt(
            role="You are a coding agent.",
            constraints=[],
            environment={},
        )
        assert "Output format" not in system
        assert "Tool guidance" not in system


class TestBuildSystemWithState:
    def test_empty_state_returns_base(self):
        base = "You are an agent."
        assert build_system_with_state(base, "Scratchpad: empty") == base

    def test_state_appended(self):
        base = "You are an agent."
        result = build_system_with_state(base, "Files read: auth.py")
        assert "Agent State" in result
        assert "auth.py" in result

In architectures with multiple specialised agents (orchestrator, coder, tester, reviewer), each agent has its own system prompt tuned to its role. System prompts are not shared — each agent's briefing is scoped to what it needs to do:

# In multi-agent systems, each agent has its own system prompt
# tuned to its specific role. They do not share a system prompt.

ORCHESTRATOR_SYSTEM = """
You are a task orchestrator. Break complex tasks into subtasks and
delegate each to the appropriate specialist agent.

For each subtask, call the delegate_task tool with:
- agent: one of "coder", "tester", "reviewer"
- task: a specific, self-contained instruction

Do not do any coding, testing, or reviewing yourself.
Synthesise the agents' results into a final answer.
"""

CODER_SYSTEM = """
You are a Python coding agent. Your job is to implement a specific
code change described in the task.

Constraints:
- Write production-quality, tested code.
- Do not modify existing tests.
- Return the complete modified file contents, not a diff.
"""

TESTER_SYSTEM = """
You are a testing agent. Given a code change, write or update
tests to cover the new behaviour.

Constraints:
- Use pytest. Do not use unittest.
- Each test must have a clear docstring describing what it tests.
- Do not modify application code.
"""

REVIEWER_SYSTEM = """
You are a code reviewer. Given a code change and its tests, identify
issues in correctness, security, and performance.

Output format:
- List each issue as: ISSUE: <category> — <description>
- After all issues, write: VERDICT: APPROVE or VERDICT: REQUEST_CHANGES
"""
— the orchestrator prompt explicitly forbids coding; the coder prompt explicitly forbids testing. This reduces role confusion in multi-turn interactions.
signal is easy to parse programmatically. Standardising output formats across agents makes the orchestrator's synthesis step simpler.
Independent system prompts are independent configurations
— changing the tester prompt does not affect the coder. Each agent can be tuned without affecting the others.

A system prompt is a configuration artefact. Small wording changes can significantly alter agent behaviour. Treating the prompt like code — with version history, notes, and a current production pointer — makes regressions traceable:

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class SystemPromptVersion:
    version: str
    system: str
    notes: str = ""


# Version your system prompts like code. Small changes can have large
# effects on agent behaviour — keeping a history makes regressions traceable.
SYSTEM_PROMPT_HISTORY: list[SystemPromptVersion] = [
    SystemPromptVersion(
        version="1.0",
        system=(
            "You are a coding agent. Fix bugs."
        ),
        notes="Initial version. Too vague — model misidentified 'bugs'.",
    ),
    SystemPromptVersion(
        version="1.1",
        system=(
            "You are a coding agent for a Python FastAPI service.\n"
            "Fix the bug described in the task.\n"
            "Do not modify test files or pyproject.toml."
        ),
        notes="Added constraint on test files. Reduced false positives.",
    ),
    SystemPromptVersion(
        version="1.2",
        system=(
            "You are a coding agent for a Python FastAPI service.\n"
            "Fix the bug described in the task. Keep changes minimal.\n\n"
            "Constraints:\n"
            "- Never modify files in tests/.\n"
            "- Never modify pyproject.toml or requirements.txt.\n"
            "- Run the test suite after every code change.\n\n"
            "Environment: Python 3.12, pytest, FastAPI 0.110"
        ),
        notes="Added run-tests constraint. Added environment. Current production version.",
    ),
]

CURRENT_SYSTEM = SYSTEM_PROMPT_HISTORY[-1].system
Commit system prompt changes to version control alongside the code changes they accompany. A diff that changes agent behaviour without a visible prompt change is hard to debug after the fact.

# PITFALL 1 — putting task-specific details in the system prompt
# System prompts are read on every call. Task details waste tokens on
# steps where they are not relevant.
BAD: system includes the full issue description and stack trace.
GOOD: task details go in messages[0] (the initial user turn).


# PITFALL 2 — contradictory constraints
# The model tries to satisfy all constraints simultaneously; contradictions
# cause unpredictable behaviour.
BAD_CONSTRAINTS = [
    "Be thorough and check every edge case.",
    "Keep your responses brief.",
    "Do not exceed 500 tokens per response.",  # conflicts with 'thorough'
]
# Pick one principle per constraint. Remove conflicts.


# PITFALL 3 — injecting secrets into the system prompt
# System prompts appear in logs, traces, and potentially cached responses.
# Never put API keys, passwords, or tokens in the system prompt.
BAD: system = f"Use API key: {API_KEY} to call the external service."
GOOD: pass secrets via environment variables; tools read them at call time.


# PITFALL 4 — over-specifying tool call order
# Telling the model exactly which tools to call and in what order reduces
# its ability to adapt when an earlier step fails or returns unexpected results.
BAD: "First call read_file, then call list_directory, then call write_file."
GOOD: "Use read_file to understand the code before writing changes."
Task details in messages, not system
— the system prompt is always-applicable background; task-specific details (issue description, file path, error message) belong in the initial user turn.
— system prompts appear in logs and traces. Pass secrets via environment variables accessed at tool-call time, not baked into the prompt string.
— specifying the exact sequence of tool calls prevents the model from adapting when an earlier step fails. Describe intent, not procedure.
The system prompt is the agent's stable briefing — passed unchanged to every

call. Structure it in up to six sections: role, constraints, environment, output format, tool guidance, and optional dynamic state. Build it from typed components using a builder function to make it easy to configure. Write concretely: name paths, versions, and exact rules rather than vague guidance. Keep it under 900 tokens for reliable API caching. Append dynamic state (scratchpad summary) as a clearly labelled final section so the stable prefix remains cacheable. Test the builder with unit tests to ensure constraints and environment details are always present.
